title: Willow square tray base
subtitle: Flat woven willow base in pairing weave, used as a display tray
excerpt: A flat square willow base in pairing weave, finished with a simple trac border to form a low-sided display tray. The base-weaving technique is the foundation of all stake-and-strand basketry.
sourceNotes: Edlin, H.L., Woodland Crafts in Britain (1949) on willow basketry. Caulfield and Saward, Dictionary of Needlework (1882) on plaited work.

glossaryTerms.definition: The central crossed-stick structure of a basket base, through which the first weavers are worked to lock the base stakes in position.
The basic willow-basket weave structure: vertical stakes form the rigid skeleton; horizontal weavers (strands) weave between them.
A two-weaver technique where two weavers alternate, each crossing in front of one stake and behind the next, alternating with the other weaver.
Willow that has been boiled to set a golden-brown colour and then stripped of bark. The standard basketry rod sold by UK suppliers.
A simple one-rod border: each stake passes in front of one upright and behind the next, then tucks inside the basket. The standard beginner border.

content.text: A flat tray base is the best introduction to
stake-and-strand
weaving: there is no curved side to manage, and the
slath
construction is visible throughout.
Buff willow
from a UK supplier soaks up reliably and is the recommended starting material. The base is woven in
pairing
weave, then finished with a
trac border
to form a low rim.
Secateurs are sharp. Close them when setting down; keep fingers clear of the cutting path.
Dry willow ends are stiff and springy when trimmed. Guard your face when snipping tall stakes.
Soaking water: do not leave willow soaking for more than 7 days. Stagnant water grows mould on the rods.
Workspace: low stable seating, knees together as a lap-board for the work-in-progress.
Materials

Buff willow rods: 20 base stakes at 3 foot length (90 cm), 2.5 to 3 mm diameter. Eight to ten pairing weavers at 4 foot length (120 cm), 2 to 2.5 mm diameter. Soak all rods in a soaking trough for 1 hour before use.
Tools
A bodkin for opening paths in the weave; a rapping iron for packing down rows; secateurs for trimming ends; a soaking trough.
Method

Lay 10 base stakes in a bundle with their butt ends pointing left. Thread the remaining 10 stakes through the centre of the bundle at right angles to form the slath: 5 stakes on each side of the centre crossing.
Open the
slath

by working two pairers around the four groups of base stakes, one pair at a time. Two rods make the pairing weavers: hold both rods together, then pass one in front of one group and behind the next group; twist the other rod over it and pass it behind the next group. Continue for two full circuits until the base stakes are spread evenly.
Open the base stakes out individually: work two more circuits of pairing, this time weaving around each stake individually. All 20 stakes should now be evenly spaced like a sun-ray pattern.
Continue
pairing
until the tray base reaches 22 cm square. Add new weavers by laying a new rod alongside the old one at a base stake and continuing with the new rod as the old one runs out.
Pack down each row with the rapping iron after every three circuits. The rapping iron drives the weavers toward the center, keeping the weave dense.

Finish with a trac border when the base is the target size: bend each base stake down in front of one upright and behind the next, then tuck it inside the last row of weaving. Work all stakes in sequence around the perimeter.
Finishing
Trim all weaver ends flush with secateurs. Let the tray dry flat on a board for 2 days. The buff willow will lighten as it dries.
Variations
Round base: start with 6 stakes pierced through 6 stakes (12 stakes total), fan them out in a circle, and pair around them.
Randing instead of pairing: single-weaver plain weave. Produces a flatter, lighter base but requires an odd number of stakes.
Natural brown willow instead of buff: slightly stiffer; soak for 3 hours rather than 1.

items.symptom: Base stakes are unevenly spaced after opening the slath
items.cause: The first two circuits of pairing did not spread the stake groups evenly
items.fix: After the first two circuits, count the spaces between stakes. If one section has twice the gap, pause and re-space that section by hand before continuing.

items.symptom: Weave is loose and the weavers shift when rapped
items.cause: Rods not packed down frequently enough
items.fix: Rap down after every two circuits, not every three. On beginner work, more frequent rapping produces a denser result.

items.symptom: Trac border stakes crack when bent down
items.cause: Willow too dry at the border stage
items.fix: Dampen the stakes with a wet cloth before bending. If the rods have dried out during weaving, re-soak for 15 minutes before attempting the border.
